text: One of the strategies to mitigate the emission of CO2 as greenhouse gas is its capture and subsequent utilisation in the synthesis of valuable chemicals such as alcohols. Due to its inert nature, the activation of CO2 is essential to enhance the reaction kinetics in its transformation into valuable chemicals. Although gold at the pure state is an inert noble metal and inactive as catalyst for hydrogenation reactions, it has the ability to be activated when deposited as nanoparticles on an appropriate oxide support. In this work, it was established through a combination of experimental and theoretical studies that, Au nanoparticles anchored and stabilised on the CeO(x)/TiO2 substrate are active for the CO2 hydrogenation at low pressure while they offer a higher selectivity to methanol. The concept of this study is centred on the localization of electronic properties and structure of catalysts for the CO2 hydrogenation reaction in order to have better selectivity to higher alcohols.
explanation: The text is highly technical and reads like a scientific abstract; while it could be human-authored, its concise, formal phrasing and specific catalyst details also resemble AI-generated science prose. Overall, the likelihood of AI authorship is moderate.
